<!--page-->
<span data-bbox="133,195,147,880" data-page="0">appearance the spectrum is very much like spectra of the Milky Way</span>
<span data-bbox="149,195,164,881" data-page="0">clouds in Sagittarius and Cygnus, and is also similar to spectra of binary</span>
<span data-bbox="167,195,180,880" data-page="0">stars of the W Ursae Majoris type, where the widening and depth of the</span>
<span data-bbox="183,194,196,755" data-page="0">lines are affected by the rapid rotation of the stars involved</span>.

<span data-bbox="200,216,213,879" data-page="0">The wide shallow absorption lines observed in the spectrum of N. G. C</span>.
<span data-bbox="216,196,230,880" data-page="0">7619 have been noticed in the spectra of other extra-galactic nebulae, and</span>
<span data-bbox="234,196,246,880" data-page="0">may be due to a dispersion in velocity and a blending of the spectral types</span>
<span data-bbox="250,195,264,881" data-page="0">of the many stars which presumably exist in the central parts of these</span>
<span data-bbox="266,196,280,882" data-page="0">nebulae. The lack of depth in the absorption lines seems to be more</span>
<span data-bbox="282,196,297,880" data-page="0">pronounced among the smaller and fainter nebulae, and in N. G. C. 7619</span>
<span data-bbox="301,195,313,459" data-page="0">the absorption is very weak</span>.

<span data-bbox="317,216,330,881" data-page="0">It is hoped that velocities of more of these interesting objects will soon</span>
<span data-bbox="334,195,345,311" data-page="0">be available</span>.

[<span data-bbox="63,882,68,926" data-page="0">Check for</span> <span data-bbox="68,885,75,922" data-page="0">updates</span> box is present on the right side of the page.]

# <span data-bbox="403,209,415,870" data-page="0">A RELATION BETWEEN DISTANCE AND RADIAL VELOCITY</span>
# <span data-bbox="420,333,432,743" data-page="0">AMONG EXTRA-GALACTIC NEBULAE</span>

<span data-bbox="446,449,457,629" data-page="0">BY EDWIN HUBBLE</span>

<span data-bbox="470,255,480,823" data-page="0">MOUNT WILSON OBSERVATORY, CARNEGIE INSTITUTION OF WASHINGTON</span>

<span data-bbox="492,401,503,668" data-page="0">Communicated January 17, 1929</span>

<span data-bbox="518,218,532,882" data-page="0">Determinations of the motion of the sun with respect to the extra</span>-
<span data-bbox="533,196,548,882" data-page="0">galactic nebulae have involved a $K$ term of several hundred kilometers</span>
<span data-bbox="550,196,567,883" data-page="0">which appears to be variable. Explanations of this paradox have been</span>
<span data-bbox="567,197,582,880" data-page="0">sought in a correlation between apparent radial velocities and distances</span>,
<span data-bbox="585,197,598,884" data-page="0">but so far the results have not been convincing. The present paper is a</span>
<span data-bbox="600,196,616,883" data-page="0">re-examination of the question, based on only those nebular distances</span>
<span data-bbox="619,197,632,558" data-page="0">which are believed to be fairly reliable</span>.

<span data-bbox="635,220,648,883" data-page="0">Distances of extra-galactic nebulae depend ultimately upon the appli</span>-
<span data-bbox="652,197,665,884" data-page="0">cation of absolute-luminosity criteria to involved stars whose types can</span>
<span data-bbox="668,197,682,882" data-page="0">be recognized. These include, among others, Cepheid variables, novae</span>,
<span data-bbox="684,198,698,883" data-page="0">and blue stars involved in emission nebulosity. Numerical values depend</span>
<span data-bbox="700,198,716,882" data-page="0">upon the zero point of the period-luminosity relation among Cepheids</span>,
<span data-bbox="717,198,732,884" data-page="0">the other criteria merely check the order of the distances. This method</span>
<span data-bbox="734,198,747,883" data-page="0">is restricted to the few nebulae which are well resolved by existing instru</span>-
<span data-bbox="750,198,764,883" data-page="0">ments. A study of these nebulae, together with those in which any stars</span>
<span data-bbox="767,198,780,885" data-page="0">at all can be recognized, indicates the probability of an approximately</span>
<span data-bbox="783,198,797,883" data-page="0">uniform upper limit to the absolute luminosity of stars, in the late-type</span>
<span data-bbox="799,198,813,854" data-page="0">spirals and irregular nebulae at least, of the order of $M$ (photographic</span>) =
-6.<span data-bbox="815,200,829,883" data-page="0">3. $^{1}$ The apparent luminosities of the brightest stars in such nebulae</span>
<span data-bbox="832,197,845,883" data-page="0">are thus criteria which, although rough and to be applied with caution</span>,

<!--page-->
<span data-bbox="135,117,151,804" data-page="1">furnish reasonable estimates of the distances of all extra-galactic systems</span>
<span data-bbox="154,118,165,507" data-page="1">in which even a few stars can be detected</span>.

<!--table: 1-->
<span data-bbox="185,410,192,478" data-page="1">TABLE 1</span>

| <span data-bbox="349,194,357,230" data-page="1">5194</span>           | <span data-bbox="335,301,344,337" data-page="1">17.3</span>  | 0.5    | + <span data-bbox="348,517,359,570" data-page="1">270</span>  | <span data-bbox="349,649,357,679" data-page="1">7.4</span>   | <span data-bbox="349,767,357,802" data-page="1">16.1</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="363,192,371,228" data-page="1">4449</span>           | <span data-bbox="363,301,371,336" data-page="1">17.8</span>  | <span data-bbox="363,407,372,443" data-page="1">0.63</span>   | + <span data-bbox="321,517,332,570" data-page="1">200</span>  | 9.5   | <span data-bbox="363,767,371,803" data-page="1">14.5</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="376,192,385,230" data-page="1">4214</span>           | <span data-bbox="377,301,385,336" data-page="1">18.3</span>  | 0.8    | + <span data-bbox="375,518,386,568" data-page="1">300</span>  | <span data-bbox="377,642,385,677" data-page="1">11.3</span>  | <span data-bbox="377,767,385,802" data-page="1">13.2</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="390,192,398,228" data-page="1">3031</span>           | 18.5  | 0.9    | - <span data-bbox="390,550,399,570" data-page="1">30</span>   | <span data-bbox="390,648,399,678" data-page="1">8.3</span>   | <span data-bbox="390,767,399,803" data-page="1">16.4</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="404,192,412,229" data-page="1">3627</span>           | 18.5  | 0.9    | + <span data-bbox="403,517,413,568" data-page="1">650</span>  | <span data-bbox="445,649,453,676" data-page="1">9.1</span>   | 15.7    |
| <span data-bbox="418,192,426,228" data-page="1">4826</span>           | 18.5  | 0.9    | + <span data-bbox="417,517,428,569" data-page="1">150</span>  | 9.0   | <span data-bbox="404,767,413,803" data-page="1">15.7</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="432,193,440,229" data-page="1">5236</span>           | <span data-bbox="390,301,399,336" data-page="1">18.5</span>  | 0.9    | + 500  | 10.4  | <span data-bbox="432,766,440,802" data-page="1">14.4</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="445,194,454,228" data-page="1">1068</span>           | <span data-bbox="445,300,454,336" data-page="1">18.7</span>  | <span data-bbox="446,408,454,434" data-page="1">1.0</span>    | + <span data-bbox="444,517,455,570" data-page="1">920</span>  | <span data-bbox="404,649,412,677" data-page="1">9.1</span>   | <span data-bbox="446,766,454,802" data-page="1">15.9</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="459,194,467,230" data-page="1">5055</span>           | 19.0  | <span data-bbox="459,408,467,434" data-page="1">1.1</span>    | + <span data-bbox="458,517,469,568" data-page="1">450</span>  | <span data-bbox="459,648,467,676" data-page="1">9.6</span>   | 15.6    |
| <span data-bbox="473,193,481,228" data-page="1">7331</span>           | <span data-bbox="459,301,467,336" data-page="1">19.0</span>  | 1.<span data-bbox="239,651,247,678" data-page="1">1    | + 5</span>00  | 10.4  | <span data-bbox="473,766,482,802" data-page="1">14.8</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="487,192,494,229" data-page="1">4258</span>           | <span data-bbox="487,301,494,336" data-page="1">19.5</span>  | <span data-bbox="487,408,495,435" data-page="1">1.4</span>    | + 500  | <span data-bbox="486,648,494,677" data-page="1">8.7</span>   | 17.0    |
| <span data-bbox="501,192,509,228" data-page="1">4151</span>           | 20.0  | <span data-bbox="501,408,509,435" data-page="1">1.7</span>    | + <span data-bbox="501,517,509,568" data-page="1">960</span>  | <span data-bbox="500,641,508,676" data-page="1">12.0</span>  | <span data-bbox="501,767,509,802" data-page="1">14.2</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="515,191,522,228" data-page="1">4382</span>           |       | 2.<span data-bbox="253,650,261,679" data-page="1">0    | + 5</span>00  | 10.0  | <span data-bbox="515,766,523,803" data-page="1">16.5</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="528,192,536,228" data-page="1">4472</span>           |       | <span data-bbox="528,406,536,435" data-page="1">2.0</span>    | + <span data-bbox="526,517,538,568" data-page="1">850</span>  | <span data-bbox="294,650,302,678" data-page="1">8.8</span>   | <span data-bbox="528,766,536,802" data-page="1">17.7</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="541,191,550,228" data-page="1">4486</span>           |       | 2.<span data-bbox="377,406,385,436" data-page="1">0    | + 8</span>00  | <span data-bbox="542,648,549,676" data-page="1">9.7</span>   | <span data-bbox="542,766,550,803" data-page="1">16.8</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="556,192,564,228" data-page="1">4649</span>           |       | <span data-bbox="515,406,523,436" data-page="1">2.0</span>    | +1090  | <span data-bbox="363,649,371,678" data-page="1">9.5</span>   | <span data-bbox="321,300,331,336" data-page="1">17.0</span>    |
| **<span data-bbox="572,117,580,163" data-page="1">Mean</span>**       |       |        |        |       | **-15.5** |
<!--end-->

<span data-bbox="591,132,603,622" data-page="1">$M_c$ = photographic magnitude of brightest stars involved</span>.
<span data-bbox="605,135,616,740" data-page="1">$r$ = distance in units of $10^6$ parsecs. The first two are Shapley's values</span>.
$v$ = <span data-bbox="618,168,629,802" data-page="1">measured velocities in km./sec. N. G. C. 6822, 221, 224 and 5457 are recent</span>
<span data-bbox="632,209,643,451" data-page="1">determinations by Humason</span>.
<span data-bbox="646,132,656,803" data-page="1">$m_e$ = Holetschek's visual magnitude as corrected by Hopmann. The first three</span>
<span data-bbox="660,209,670,801" data-page="1">objects were not measured by Holetschek, and the values of $m_e$ represent</span>
<span data-bbox="673,209,684,727" data-page="1">estimates by the author based upon such data as are available</span>.
$M_c$ = <span data-bbox="688,197,698,651" data-page="1">total visual absolute magnitude computed from $m_e$ and $r</span>$.

<span data-bbox="718,138,731,804" data-page="1">Finally, the nebulae themselves appear to be of a definite order of</span>
<span data-bbox="735,117,748,802" data-page="1">absolute luminosity, exhibiting a range of four or five magnitudes about</span>
<span data-bbox="749,116,766,802" data-page="1">an average value $M$ (visual) = -15.2.$^{1}$ The application of this statistical</span>
<span data-bbox="767,116,782,801" data-page="1">average to individual cases can rarely be used to advantage, but where</span>
<span data-bbox="785,116,797,801" data-page="1">considerable numbers are involved, and especially in the various clusters</span>
<span data-bbox="799,117,814,802" data-page="1">of nebulae, mean apparent luminosities of the nebulae themselves offer</span>
<span data-bbox="817,116,828,489" data-page="1">reliable estimates of the mean distances</span>.

<span data-bbox="834,138,846,802" data-page="1">Radial velocities of 46 extra-galactic nebulae are now available, but</span>

<!--page-->
<span data-bbox="129,196,143,882" data-page="2">individual distances are estimated for only 24. For one other, N. G. C</span>.
<span data-bbox="146,197,159,882" data-page="2">3521, an estimate could probably be made, but no photographs are avail</span>-
<span data-bbox="162,197,176,883" data-page="2">able at Mount Wilson. The data are given in table 1. The first seven</span>
<span data-bbox="179,197,192,885" data-page="2">distances are the most reliable, depending, except for M 32 the companion of</span>
<span data-bbox="196,197,209,883" data-page="2">M 31, upon extensive investigations of many stars involved. The next</span>
<span data-bbox="212,197,226,883" data-page="2">thirteen distances, depending upon the criterion of a uniform upper limit</span>
<span data-bbox="229,197,242,883" data-page="2">of stellar luminosity, are subject to considerable probable errors but are</span>
<span data-bbox="245,197,259,883" data-page="2">believed to be the most reasonable values at present available. The last</span>
<span data-bbox="262,197,275,883" data-page="2">four objects appear to be in the Virgo Cluster. The distance assigned</span>
<span data-bbox="278,197,291,883" data-page="2">to the cluster, $2 \times 10^6$ parsecs, is derived from the distribution of nebular</span>
<span data-bbox="295,197,308,884" data-page="2">luminosities, together with luminosities of stars in some of the later-type</span>
<span data-bbox="311,198,325,884" data-page="2">spirals, and differs somewhat from the Harvard estimate of ten million</span>
<span data-bbox="328,197,342,309" data-page="2">light years. $^{2}$</span>

<span data-bbox="345,220,356,883" data-page="2">The data in the table indicate a linear correlation between distances and</span>
<span data-bbox="362,198,375,883" data-page="2">velocities, whether the latter are used directly or corrected for solar motion</span>,
<span data-bbox="376,199,393,884" data-page="2">according to the older solutions. This suggests a new solution for the solar</span>
<span data-bbox="395,199,406,883" data-page="2">motion in which the distances are introduced as coefficients of the $K$ term</span>,
<span data-bbox="411,198,425,885" data-page="2">i. e., the velocities are assumed to vary directly with the distances, and</span>
<span data-bbox="426,198,442,885" data-page="2">hence $K$ represents the velocity at unit distance due to this effect. The</span>
<span data-bbox="444,199,458,582" data-page="2">equations of condition then take the form</span>

<span data-bbox="473,295,488,787" data-page="2">$rK + X \cos \alpha \cos \delta + Y \sin \alpha \cos \delta + Z \sin \delta = v$</span>.

<span data-bbox="503,200,516,885" data-page="2">Two solutions have been made, one using the 24 nebulae individually</span>,
<span data-bbox="519,199,532,885" data-page="2">the other combining them into 9 groups according to proximity in direc</span>-
<span data-bbox="536,200,547,552" data-page="2">tion and in distance. The results are</span>

<!--table: 2-->
|              | **<span data-bbox="564,385,572,458" data-page="2">24 OBJECTS</span>** | **<span data-bbox="564,549,571,611" data-page="2">9 GROUPS</span>** |
| :----------- | :------------- | :----------- |
| $X$          | -65 ± 50       | +3 ± 70      |
| $Y$          | +226 ± 95      | +230 ± 120   |
| $Z$          | -195 ± 40      | -<span data-bbox="604,530,613,635" data-page="2">133 ± 70</span>    |
| $K$          | +<span data-bbox="618,369,629,412" data-page="2">465</span> ± 50      | +<span data-bbox="617,531,629,832" data-page="2">513 ± 60 km./sec. per $10^6$ parsecs</span>. |
| $\Lambda$    | <span data-bbox="638,383,647,421" data-page="2">286</span>°           | <span data-bbox="637,545,646,582" data-page="2">269</span>°         |
| $D$          | +40°           | +33°         |
| $V_0$        | <span data-bbox="665,384,676,483" data-page="2">306 km./sec</span>.   | <span data-bbox="665,545,675,645" data-page="2">247 km./sec</span>. |
<!--end-->

<span data-bbox="692,223,708,888" data-page="2">For such scanty material, so poorly distributed, the results are fairly</span>
<span data-bbox="710,202,722,888" data-page="2">definite. Differences between the two solutions are due largely to the</span>
<span data-bbox="725,201,740,888" data-page="2">four Virgo nebulae, which, being the most distant objects and all sharing</span>
<span data-bbox="741,202,758,888" data-page="2">the peculiar motion of the cluster, unduly influence the value of $K$ and</span>
<span data-bbox="758,202,773,888" data-page="2">hence of $V_0$. New data on more distant objects will be required to reduce</span>
<span data-bbox="774,202,790,888" data-page="2">the effect of such peculiar motion. Meanwhile round numbers, inter</span>-
<span data-bbox="791,202,806,889" data-page="2">mediate between the two solutions, will represent the probable order of</span>
the values. For instance, let $\Lambda$ = 277°, $D$ = +36° (Gal. long. = 32°,
<span data-bbox="823,197,839,887" data-page="2">lat. = +18°), $V_0$ = 280 km./sec., $K$ = +500 km./sec. per million par</span>-

<!--page-->
<span data-bbox="137,121,149,808" data-page="3">secs. Mr. Strömberg has very kindly checked the general order of these</span>
<span data-bbox="153,121,167,743" data-page="3">values by independent solutions for different groupings of the data</span>.

<span data-bbox="170,142,183,807" data-page="3">A constant term, introduced into the equations, was found to be small</span>
<span data-bbox="187,121,200,807" data-page="3">and negative. This seems to dispose of the necessity for the old constant</span>
<span data-bbox="203,120,216,807" data-page="3">$K$ term. Solutions of this sort have been published by Lundmark,$^{3}$ who</span>
<span data-bbox="216,121,232,806" data-page="3">replaced the old $K$ by $k + lr + mr^2$. His favored solution gave $k$ = 513</span>,
<span data-bbox="236,120,249,807" data-page="3">as against the former value of the order of 700, and hence offered little</span>
<span data-bbox="253,121,266,220" data-page="3">advantage</span>.

<!--table: 3-->
<span data-bbox="277,413,284,482" data-page="3">TABLE 2</span>
<span data-bbox="291,156,300,760" data-page="3">NEBULAE WHOSE DISTANCES ARE ESTIMATED FROM RADIAL VELOCITIES</span>

| <span data-bbox="307,197,313,243" data-page="3">OBJECT</span>        | $v$    | $v_0$ | $r$    | $m_e$ | <span data-bbox="307,662,315,682" data-page="3">$M$</span>     |
| :------------ | :----- | :---- | :----- | :---- | :------ |
| <span data-bbox="318,121,328,232" data-page="3">N. G. C. 278</span>  | + 650  | -110  | 1.52   | 12.0  | -13.9   |
| <span data-bbox="333,205,340,233" data-page="3">404</span>           | - 25   | - 65  |        | 11.1  |         |
| <span data-bbox="347,206,354,233" data-page="3">584</span>           | +1800  | + 75  | <span data-bbox="346,545,354,583" data-page="3">3.45</span>   | 10.9  | 16.8    |
| <span data-bbox="359,205,368,232" data-page="3">936</span>           | +1300  | +<span data-bbox="359,433,369,475" data-page="3">115</span>  | <span data-bbox="360,546,368,584" data-page="3">2.37</span>   | 11.1  | 15.7    |
| <span data-bbox="374,197,382,233" data-page="3">1023</span>          | + 300  | - 10  | 0.62   | 10.2  | 13.8    |
| 1700          | + 800  | +<span data-bbox="389,433,399,475" data-page="3">220</span>  | 1.16   | 12.5  | 12.8    |
| <span data-bbox="404,196,412,232" data-page="3">2681</span>          | + <span data-bbox="403,307,413,357" data-page="3">700</span>  | - 10  | 1.42   | 10.7  | 15.0    |
| <span data-bbox="418,196,426,233" data-page="3">2683</span>          | + <span data-bbox="417,308,427,357" data-page="3">400</span>  | + 65  | 0.67   | 9.9   | 14.3    |
| <span data-bbox="432,196,440,233" data-page="3">2841</span>          | + 600  | - 20  | 1.24   | 9.4   | 16.1    |
| <span data-bbox="445,196,453,234" data-page="3">3034</span>          | + <span data-bbox="444,307,455,358" data-page="3">290</span>  | -<span data-bbox="461,434,470,476" data-page="3">105</span>  | 0.79   | <span data-bbox="445,663,453,691" data-page="3">9.0</span>   | 15.5    |
| <span data-bbox="461,196,470,233" data-page="3">3115</span>          | + 600  | +<span data-bbox="444,432,454,476" data-page="3">105</span>  | 1.00   | <span data-bbox="461,661,469,691" data-page="3">9.5</span>   | 15.5    |
| <span data-bbox="475,196,484,233" data-page="3">3368</span>          | + <span data-bbox="476,308,485,359" data-page="3">940</span>  | + 70  | 1.74   | 10.0  | 16.2    |
| <span data-bbox="489,196,498,232" data-page="3">3379</span>          | + <span data-bbox="488,307,499,358" data-page="3">810</span>  | + 65  | 1.49   | 9.4   | 16.4    |
| <span data-bbox="503,196,511,234" data-page="3">3489</span>          | + <span data-bbox="431,307,441,359" data-page="3">600</span>  | + 50  | 1.10   | 11.2  | 14.0    |
| <span data-bbox="519,196,528,232" data-page="3">3521</span>          | + <span data-bbox="519,307,530,358" data-page="3">730</span>  | + <span data-bbox="547,456,555,475" data-page="3">95</span>  | 1.27   | 10.1  | 15.4    |
| <span data-bbox="533,196,542,233" data-page="3">3623</span>          | + 800  | + 35  | 1.53   | 9.9   | 16.0    |
| <span data-bbox="548,196,555,232" data-page="3">4111</span>          | + 800  | - <span data-bbox="519,432,529,474" data-page="3">95</span>  | 1.79   | 10.1  | 16.1    |
| <span data-bbox="561,196,569,233" data-page="3">4526</span>          | + <span data-bbox="560,306,571,358" data-page="3">580</span>  | - 20  | 1.20   | 11.1  | 14.3    |
| <span data-bbox="577,196,586,233" data-page="3">4565</span>          | +<span data-bbox="318,432,327,475" data-page="3">110</span>0  | - 75  | 2.<span data-bbox="532,432,543,475" data-page="3">35</span>   | 11.0  | 15.9    |
| <span data-bbox="592,196,599,233" data-page="3">4594</span>          | +<span data-bbox="591,307,600,358" data-page="3">1140</span>  | + 25  | 2.23   | 9.1   | <span data-bbox="590,771,599,806" data-page="3">17.6</span>    |
| <span data-bbox="605,197,613,232" data-page="3">5005</span>          | + <span data-bbox="604,307,615,358" data-page="3">900</span>  | -130  | 2.06   | 11.1  | 15.5    |
| <span data-bbox="619,197,627,233" data-page="3">5866</span>          | + 650  | -<span data-bbox="618,432,627,475" data-page="3">215</span>  | 1.73   | 11.7  | -14.5   |
| **<span data-bbox="636,121,644,167" data-page="3">Mean</span>**      |        |       |        | **10.5** | **-15.3** |
<!--end-->

<span data-bbox="666,143,678,807" data-page="3">The residuals for the two solutions given above average 150 and 110</span>
<span data-bbox="682,122,696,806" data-page="3">km./sec. and should represent the average peculiar motions of the in</span>-
<span data-bbox="699,122,712,807" data-page="3">dividual nebulae and of the groups, respectively. In order to exhibit</span>
<span data-bbox="714,122,729,806" data-page="3">the results in a graphical form, the solar motion has been eliminated from</span>
<span data-bbox="731,122,744,807" data-page="3">the observed velocities and the remainders, the distance terms plus the</span>
<span data-bbox="748,122,761,805" data-page="3">residuals, have been plotted against the distances. The run of the re</span>-
<span data-bbox="765,122,778,810" data-page="3">siduals is about as smooth as can be expected, and in general the form of</span>
<span data-bbox="781,122,796,464" data-page="3">the solutions appears to be adequate</span>.

<span data-bbox="798,143,808,808" data-page="3">The 22 nebulae for which distances are not available can be treated in</span>
<span data-bbox="813,121,830,808" data-page="3">two ways. First, the mean distance of the group derived from the mean</span>
<span data-bbox="829,122,846,805" data-page="3">apparent magnitudes can be compared with the mean of the velocities</span>

<!--page-->
<span data-bbox="135,197,149,884" data-page="4">corrected for solar motion. The result, 745 km./sec. for a distance of</span>
<span data-bbox="151,199,166,882" data-page="4">$1.4 \times 10^6$ parsecs, falls between the two previous solutions and indicates</span>
<span data-bbox="169,197,182,797" data-page="4">a value for $K$ of 530 as against the proposed value, 500 km./sec</span>.

<span data-bbox="186,218,199,883" data-page="4">Secondly, the scatter of the individual nebulae can be examined by</span>
<span data-bbox="203,197,216,883" data-page="4">assuming the relation between distances and velocities as previously</span>
<span data-bbox="220,197,231,882" data-page="4">determined. Distances can then be calculated from the velocities cor</span>-
<span data-bbox="237,196,249,883" data-page="4">rected for solar motion, and absolute magnitudes can be derived from the</span>
<span data-bbox="253,197,266,884" data-page="4">apparent magnitudes. The results are given in table 2 and may be</span>
<span data-bbox="270,197,283,883" data-page="4">compared with the distribution of absolute magnitudes among the nebulae</span>
<span data-bbox="286,196,299,883" data-page="4">in table 1, whose distances are derived from other criteria. N. G. C. 404</span>

<!--figure: 1-->
<span data-bbox="531,499,539,575" data-page="4">FIGURE 1</span>
**<span data-bbox="544,304,555,771" data-page="4">Velocity-Distance Relation among Extra-Galactic Nebulae</span>.**
A scatter plot showing radial velocities (y-axis, from 0 to +<span data-bbox="356,269,361,313" data-page="4">1000 KM</span>) against distance (x-axis, from 0 to $2 \times 10^6$ PARSECS). Black discs and a solid line represent the solution for solar motion using individual nebulae. Open circles and a dashed line represent the solution combining nebulae into groups. A cross symbol marks the mean velocity corresponding to <span data-bbox="645,271,654,806" data-page="4">the mean distance of 22 nebulae whose distances could not be esti</span><span data-bbox="658,271,669,426" data-page="4">mated individually</span>. The solid line, representing individual nebulae, starts near the origin and increases linearly with distance. The dashed line, for grouped nebulae, shows a similar linear increase but with a slightly steeper slope.
<!--end-->

<span data-bbox="690,197,703,883" data-page="4">can be excluded, since the observed velocity is so small that the peculiar</span>
<span data-bbox="706,197,719,882" data-page="4">motion must be large in comparison with the distance effect. The object</span>
<span data-bbox="723,197,736,882" data-page="4">is not necessarily an exception, however, since a distance can be assigned</span>
<span data-bbox="740,196,752,883" data-page="4">for which the peculiar motion and the absolute magnitude are both within</span>
<span data-bbox="752,196,771,883" data-page="4">the range previously determined. The two mean magnitudes, -15.3</span>
<span data-bbox="770,197,786,882" data-page="4">and -15.5, the ranges, 4.9 and 5.0 mag., and the frequency distributions</span>
<span data-bbox="788,197,802,883" data-page="4">are closely similar for these two entirely independent sets of data; and</span>
<span data-bbox="804,196,818,883" data-page="4">even the slight difference in mean magnitudes can be attributed to the</span>
<span data-bbox="820,197,834,882" data-page="4">selected, very bright, nebulae in the Virgo Cluster. This entirely unforced</span>
<span data-bbox="836,197,851,883" data-page="4">agreement supports the validity of the velocity-distance relation in a very</span>

<!--page-->
<span data-bbox="137,120,151,807" data-page="5">evident matter. Finally, it is worth recording that the frequency distribu</span>-
<span data-bbox="153,120,167,806" data-page="5">tion of absolute magnitudes in the two tables combined is comparable</span>
<span data-bbox="170,120,180,594" data-page="5">with those found in the various clusters of nebulae</span>. $^{4}$

<span data-bbox="187,142,199,805" data-page="5">The results establish a roughly linear relation between velocities and</span>
<span data-bbox="203,120,217,804" data-page="5">distances among nebulae for which velocities have been previously pub</span>-
<span data-bbox="220,120,233,803" data-page="5">lished, and the relation appears to dominate the distribution of velocities</span>.
<span data-bbox="237,120,249,805" data-page="5">In order to investigate the matter on a much larger scale, Mr. Humason</span>
<span data-bbox="253,120,266,805" data-page="5">at Mount Wilson has initiated a program of determining velocities</span>
<span data-bbox="269,120,280,806" data-page="5">of the most distant nebulae that can be observed with confidence</span>.
<span data-bbox="286,120,299,805" data-page="5">These, naturally, are the brightest nebulae in clusters of nebulae</span>.
<span data-bbox="301,121,316,807" data-page="5">The first definite result, $v$ = + 3779 km./sec. for N. G. C. 7619, is</span>
<span data-bbox="318,119,332,806" data-page="5">thoroughly consistent with the present conclusions. Corrected for the</span>
<span data-bbox="334,121,349,805" data-page="5">solar motion, this velocity is +3910, which, with $K$ = 500, corresponds to</span>
<span data-bbox="350,120,365,803" data-page="5">a distance of $7.8 \times 10^6$ parsecs. Since the apparent magnitude is 11.8</span>,
<span data-bbox="369,120,381,807" data-page="5">the absolute magnitude at such a distance is -17.65, which is of the</span>
<span data-bbox="385,120,398,805" data-page="5">right order for the brightest nebulae in a cluster. A preliminary dis</span>-
<span data-bbox="402,121,415,804" data-page="5">tance, derived independently from the cluster of which this nebula appears</span>
<span data-bbox="416,120,431,591" data-page="5">to be a member, is of the order of $7 \times 10^6$ parsecs</span>.

<span data-bbox="435,142,448,805" data-page="5">New data to be expected in the near future may modify the significance</span>
<span data-bbox="451,120,464,806" data-page="5">of the present investigation or, if confirmatory, will lead to a solution</span>
<span data-bbox="467,120,481,804" data-page="5">having many times the weight. For this reason it is thought premature</span>
<span data-bbox="484,120,497,806" data-page="5">to discuss in detail the obvious consequences of the present results. For</span>
<span data-bbox="501,120,514,805" data-page="5">example, if the solar motion with respect to the clusters represents the</span>
<span data-bbox="517,120,530,806" data-page="5">rotation of the galactic system, this motion could be subtracted from the</span>
<span data-bbox="534,120,547,807" data-page="5">results for the nebulae and the remainder would represent the motion of</span>
<span data-bbox="550,120,563,691" data-page="5">the galactic system with respect to the extra-galactic nebulae</span>.

<span data-bbox="567,140,580,805" data-page="5">The outstanding feature, however, is the possibility that the velocity</span>-
<span data-bbox="584,120,596,804" data-page="5">distance relation may represent the de Sitter effect, and hence that numer</span>-
<span data-bbox="600,119,613,806" data-page="5">ical data may be introduced into discussions of the general curvature of</span>
<span data-bbox="617,119,630,805" data-page="5">space. In the de Sitter cosmology, displacements of the spectra arise</span>
<span data-bbox="631,119,646,805" data-page="5">from two sources, an apparent slowing down of atomic vibrations and a</span>
<span data-bbox="648,119,663,805" data-page="5">general tendency of material particles to scatter. The latter involves an</span>
<span data-bbox="666,119,677,804" data-page="5">acceleration and hence introduces the element of time. The relative im</span>-
<span data-bbox="681,119,696,805" data-page="5">portance of these two effects should determine the form of the relation</span>
<span data-bbox="699,119,711,805" data-page="5">between distances and observed velocities; and in this connection it may</span>
<span data-bbox="716,119,729,805" data-page="5">be emphasized that the linear relation found in the present discussion is a</span>
<span data-bbox="732,119,746,697" data-page="5">first approximation representing a restricted range in distance</span>.

<span data-bbox="753,142,764,712" data-page="5">1.  Mt. Wilson Contr., No. 324; Astroph. J., Chicago, Ill., 64, 1926 (321</span>).
2.  <span data-bbox="767,155,777,436" data-page="5">Harvard Coll. Obs. Circ., 294, 1926</span>.
3.  <span data-bbox="781,156,791,516" data-page="5">Mon. Not. R. Astr. Soc., 85, 1925 (865-894</span>).
<span data-bbox="794,143,804,451" data-page="5">4.  These PROCEEDINGS, 15, 1929 (167</span>).
